content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 10, 2015

Anthony P. Condotti
City Attorney
City of Santa Cruz
333 Church Street
Santa Cruz, CA 95060

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-15-190

Dear Mr. Condotti:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Planning Commissioner Mark
Primack regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 We
are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71) and we do not
advise on past conduct.

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit Commissioner Primack from representing a client before the City's
Board of Building Appeals?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit Commissioner Primack's representation provided that he is
not acting or appearing to act on behalf of the Planning Commission or as a Planning Commissioner
when he represents his client.

FACTS

You are the City Attorney for the City of Santa Cruz and you write on behalf of Planning
Commissioner Mark Primack. Commissioner Primack is a licensed architect with clients in Santa
Cruz as well as outside the city. Commissioner Primack submitted plans (the "Plans") for a remodel

1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-15-190
Page No. 2

project on behalf of "Soif Wine Bar, Restaurant & Merchants," a local business that includes a
restaurant, wine bar, and "off-sale" retail wine sales. Among other things, the plans proposed to
modify the business's interior by relocating the retail area devoted to "off-sale" wine purchases into
a newly created suite next to the existing location, and to increase the assembly area devoted to
restaurant and wine bar patrons. Initial plans sought to retain an existing, inward swinging exit door
and installation of a new bar area and table seating in the area formerly used for retail wine sales.
After Commissioner Primack's staff submitted revised plans based on interactions with the City, the
City issued a building permit on June 17, 2015.

Commissioner Primack and the City disagreed about aspects of the plans. Commissioner
Primack's staff submitted a Building Permit Revision Application (the "Revision Application") to
modify the approved plans to retain the existing inward-swinging exit door and to provide wheelchair-
accessible seating in an alternate location. Included was a letter attaching a proposed "code path,"
i.e., a detailed analysis of the pertinent Building Code provisions intended to demonstrate that the
revised plans meet applicable requirements. The City rejected that application and notified
Commissioner Primack and his client of the right to appeal the Building Official and Fire Marshal's
determinations to the City's Board of Building Appeals.

Santa Cruz's Board of Building and Fire Appeals ("Board") hear and decide appeals of
orders, decisions or determinations made by the chief building official and/or the fire marshal
regarding the application and interpretation of building and construction codes as well as fire
prevention codes. The Board is a decision-making body separate from the City's Planning
Commission. It does not have a staff or regular meetings, rather it meets only as needed to hear
appeals like the one described here (on average, one or two times per year). If a person files an
appeal, the Board's Chairperson is notified and convenes the Board. The Board hears the matter and
issues its finding. The findings can then be challenged by an administrative writ in a superior court.

Commissioner Primack may appear before or submit supporting materials to the Board to
represent his client and his plan.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any state or local public official from making, participating in
making, or using his or her official position to influence a government decision in which the official
has a financial interest specified in Section 87103. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
government decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section
87103.) An issue is not whether there might be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
Commissioner Primack's interest in his business or his client as a source of income, but whether he
will be making, participating in, or influencing a decision under the Act. For this reason, we start
our analysis on this point.

An official "makes" a governmental decision when he or she: "authorizes or directs any
action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or
enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency" (Regulation 18704(a).) An
official "participates in" a governmental decision when he or she: "provides information, an
opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting the decision without significant

File No. A-15-190
Page No. 3

intervening substantive review." (Regulation 18704(b).) Neither of these is present here.
Commissioner Primack is neither making nor participating in making a decision of his own
government body.

A public official "uses his or her official position to influence" a governmental decision if
he or she:

"(1) Contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in an agency
subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her agency for the purpose
of affecting a decision; or

"(2) Contacts or appears before any official in any other government agency for
the purpose of affecting a decision, and the public official acts or purports to act
within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the
contact." (Regulation 18704(c).)

On the first point, Commissioner Primack is not appearing before his own agency and you
have stated that the Board is not "subject to the authority or budgetary control" of the Planning
Commission. Both entities are, however, under the umbrella of the City's Department of
Community Development. We have previously found that contact with shared staff between the
official's own agency and the agency before which he or she will appear (or with which he or she
will communicate) could amount to influencing one's own agency. (See e.g. Watson Advice letter,
No. A-14-104.) The Board, however, does not have a staff and only meets on an as-needed basis.

On the second point, a public official influences a decision if he contacts or appears before
another government agency, as here. Commissioner Primack will be appearing before the Board, a
separate government entity. Based on your facts, the Commissioner is not acting in his official
capacity within the authority of his position when appearing in front of the Board, but is acting in
his private capacity on behalf of an architectural client. The Act considers a public official to be
"influencing" a decision if he or she acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the representative of,
his or her agency to any member, officer, employee, or consultant of another agency. We have said,
for example, that referring to one's office or using official stationery (from the Planning
Commission, in this case) to communicate with the other agency would be "purporting to use his or
her official position." (See id.)

Provided that Commissioner Primack does not present himself as acting for or on behalf of
the Planning Commission, he is not considered to be "making a decision" under the Act, and his
appearance is not prohibited.

File No. A-15-190
Page No. 4


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[handwritten signature]

By:    Heather M. Rowan
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

HMR:jgl
